method3.generated_article:
==Volcano and caldera==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai is the emergent part of the Hunga submarine volcano, which lies on the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[4] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1.8 kilometres from the seafloor, extends for about 20 kilometres across, and is mostly submerged.[4] Its summit contains a large caldera about 5 kilometres in diameter, of which only the northern and western rims historically stood above sea level as the uninhabited islands of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai.[4]

Early geological descriptions interpreted Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai as remnants of the rim of a larger submarine caldera.[2] Bryan and colleagues described the islands as elongated features tangent to a circle centred on a rocky shoal south of Hunga Tonga, and rock samples from Hunga Tonga showed alternating layers of andesitic lava flows with scoria, lapilli, and ash dipping gently away from the caldera centre.[2] Later high-resolution multibeam sonar surveys confirmed a central submarine depression about 4 by 2 kilometres wide and 150 metres deep, consistent with collapse during an earlier explosive eruption.[2]

The volcano's eruptive products are predominantly andesitic, a composition typical of intra-oceanic arc volcanoes.[4] Historical eruptions were recorded at the Hunga volcano before the 21st century, including activity in 1912 and 1937 near the rocky shoal identified in earlier studies.[2]

In December 2014 and January 2015, an eruption built a tephra cone that linked the previously separate islands into a single larger island named Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai.[4] By late 2015 and even into November 2021, despite some shoreline erosion and settling, the new landform remained broadly similar to its post-eruption configuration.[2]

The island created in 2015 was small and short-lived, covering just over 1 square kilometre and reaching a maximum elevation of 149 metres before 2022.[5] On 15 January 2022, the volcano produced the largest underwater explosion ever recorded by modern scientific instruments and the most explosive volcanic event of the satellite era.[1][3] The eruption has been described as a once-in-1,000-years event for the Hunga caldera and may rank as high as 5 on the Volcanic Explosivity Index, although the final estimate depends on the total erupted volume.[5]

The 2022 eruption destroyed the 2015 land bridge and cone, leaving only small remnants of Hunga Tonga and Hunga Haʻapai above sea level.[4][5] The blast also generated shock waves measured globally, and one geophysical study cited in popular reporting described it as the biggest natural explosion ever recorded in the instrumental era.[3] In addition to reshaping the caldera rim, the eruption injected enough water vapour into the stratosphere to increase global stratospheric water by about 10 percent.[1][3]

==Geography==
Hunga Tonga–Hunga Haʻapai lies in the central part of the Tongan archipelago, about 65 km north of Tongatapu, the country's main island, and roughly 30 km south of Fonuafoʻou.[4] The volcano is part of the Tonga segment of the Tonga–Kermadec intra-oceanic arc, formed by subduction of the Pacific Plate beneath the Indo-Australian Plate.[5]

Geographically, the feature is the emergent portion of a much larger submarine volcano that rises more than 1.8 km from the seafloor and spans about 20 km across at its base.[5] Near its summit is a caldera approximately 5 km in diameter, of which only the highest rim segments historically stood above sea level.[5]

Before the 2022 eruption, the joined islands covered just over 1 km2 and reached a maximum elevation of 149 m above sea level.[4] Satellite observations showed that this landmass consisted largely of unconsolidated tephra and was subject to rapid coastal and morphological change.[1][5]

The volcano's isolated oceanic setting did not prevent it from producing effects across a wide region.[1][4] The January 2022 eruption generated pressure waves detected around the world, with shockwaves felt in Fiji, New Zealand and as far away as Alaska, and atmospheric waves reportedly circling the globe three times before dissipating.[1] The same event also produced a Pacific-wide tsunami that reached many coastlines around the Pacific basin, including parts of the Americas and northeast Asia.[4]

Ashfall from the 2022 eruption was distributed across several island groups in Tonga, including Haʻapai, Tongatapu and ʻEua.[4] On Tongatapu, reported ash thickness was about 20–30 mm, while deposits elsewhere in Tonga ranged from about 5 to 50 mm.[4]

==Eruptions==
The volcano’s 15 January 2022 paroxysm began at about 5 p.m. local time and rapidly intensified into a violent explosive eruption.[4] The eruption column rose about 36 miles (58 km) into the atmosphere, while satellite analysis described a vertical plume more than 30 km high with overshooting tops above 55 km, the highest observed in the satellite era.[4][5]

Because the vent lay only about 200 metres below sea level, large volumes of seawater interacted directly with magma, greatly increasing explosivity.[1][3] NASA researchers described the event as beyond a typical Surtseyan eruption and informally termed it an “ultra Surtseyan” eruption, possibly triggered when collapse of part of the caldera rim allowed seawater to rush into a hot magma chamber.[3] The initial explosion released an estimated 10–28 exajoules of energy, probably exceeding the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo and possibly approaching that of Krakatoa in 1883.[5]

The eruption generated destructive tsunamis in Tonga, with run-up heights reported as high as 65 feet (20 m), destroying hundreds of structures, displacing more than 1,500 people, and causing four deaths in the kingdom.[4] Tsunami waves also propagated across the Pacific, causing two further deaths in Peru and damage in places including Fiji, Hawaiʻi, Chile, California, Japan, Russia, and New Zealand.[4]

The eruption also caused major geomorphic and submarine changes around the volcano.[3][4] Surveys reported fine sandy mud, ash ripples up to 50 kilometres from the vent, gouged valleys, and huge sediment accumulations on the seafloor, while Tonga’s domestic internet cable was buried beneath about 100 feet of sediment and ash.[4] Scientists estimated that as much as one and two-thirds cubic miles of material was displaced during the event.[4]

Monitoring the eruption sequence was hampered by the lack of nearby instruments, as the nearest functioning seismic station was nearly 500 miles from the volcano.[4] In response, the USGS National Earthquake Information Center and the Volcano Disaster Assistance Program developed near-real-time methods to identify otherwise undetected earthquakes near the volcano in the days and weeks after the eruption.[4]

Atmospherically, the eruption was unusual because it injected both a moderate amount of sulfur dioxide and an exceptionally large quantity of water vapour into the stratosphere.[1] Subsequent studies found that stratospheric water content increased by about 10 percent, an unprecedented rise for the modern observing era.[2] Although the water vapour prompted initial concern that the eruption might warm the climate, sulfate aerosols formed from sulfur dioxide reflected incoming sunlight and produced a net cooling effect.[1][2] A UCLA-led study concluded that the eruption reduced Southern Hemisphere temperatures by about 0.1 °C and, overall, caused slight global cooling from 2022, though some evidence suggested a small longer-term warming influence in the Northern Hemisphere because stratospheric water vapour can persist for years.[1]

==Footnotes==
Some early commentary attributed the exceptional violence of the January 2022 eruption in part to a possible submarine landslide that may have removed overlying rock and rapidly decompressed the volcanic system, though this was presented as an explanatory hypothesis rather than a settled conclusion.[2] Geological discussion of the event also renewed attention to evidence for much older, larger tsunami-generating volcanic collapses in Tonga, including a probable mid-15th-century event with run-up heights of about 30 metres.[2][3]

The eruption prompted extensive post-event field, stratigraphic, and modelling work, with researchers emphasizing the need to document fresh tsunami deposits in Tonga before they are altered by human activity or subsequent storms.[3] Reviews of the disaster further argued that the event exposed important gaps in understanding coupled atmospheric and oceanic tsunami generation, especially the role of atmospheric pressure waves and resonance in producing unusual far-field tsunami behaviour across the Pacific.[3]

Subsequent studies also examined the eruption’s atmospheric consequences in more detail, particularly its unusually large injection of water vapour and comparatively modest sulfate aerosol burden.[4][5] Satellite-based analyses reported that the eruption produced contrasting temperature responses in the stratosphere, with cooling in the mid-stratosphere and warming in the lower stratosphere that persisted for more than a year.[4] Later assessments found that, despite initial expectations of short-term warming, the net radiative effect included measurable cooling over the Southern Hemisphere of about 0.1 °C by late 2022 and 2023.[5]

Data from one major geochemical and oceanographic study of the eruption’s marine aftermath were deposited in the PANGAEA database and Figshare, with supplementary material published alongside the article.[1] That study described the volcano as lying about 150 metres below sea level before the climactic eruption and noted that the 15 January 2022 event was among the largest volcanic episodes recorded with modern geophysical instruments.[1]
